---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины / каротажная диаграмма)** — specifically a composite well log or drilling record from a Soviet-era exploration borehole. It appears to be a continuous strip log running vertically down the page, combining lithological/stratigraphic description columns with one or more **geophysical wireline curves** (most prominently what appears to be a **self-potential (SP) or resistivity curve** running as a continuous oscillating trace down the centre of the log).

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a **single borehole** drilled during the regional survey described in Report 36572. The header text at the top references **"УЧН КОПИТОМОСВАНА"** or similar (partially illegible due to scan quality), likely a site or borehole designation. The log appears to extend over a considerable depth interval — based on the length of the strip and typical Soviet exploration borehole formats, likely covering several hundred metres (possibly **0–500 m or deeper**), though exact depth values at top and bottom are difficult to read at this resolution.

The bottom of the log contains the label **"КОНТРОЛЬНОЙ ЗОНДЫ"** (control probe/sonde), suggesting the geophysical curve shown is from a standard electrical logging tool.

---

## Key Content

The log is structured as a **multi-column composite strip log**, typical of Soviet borehole documentation. The following columns are identifiable from left to right, though labels are partially illegible:

1. **Far left columns**: Likely contain **depth intervals (м — metres)**, **core recovery or sample numbers**, and **stratigraphic/formation codes** — small numbered or lettered boxes are visible at irregular intervals suggesting lithological unit boundaries.

2. **Left-centre columns**: Appear to contain **lithological description text** (written in Russian, illegible at this scan resolution) and possibly **sample or interval notation**.

3. **Centre column (dominant feature)**: A continuous **oscillating geophysical curve** — this is the most visually prominent element. The curve shows:
   - Relatively **high-frequency, moderate-amplitude oscillations** through most of the log
   - Several zones of **notably larger amplitude deflections** (anomalous intervals) interspersed throughout
   - At least one section in the lower third of the log where the curve becomes **notably more irregular and high-amplitude**, possibly indicating a zone of interest (e.g., mineralised, fractured, or lithologically variable interval)
   - The curve appears to deflect both left and right of a central baseline, consistent with **SP (самопроизвольная поляризация / self-potential)** or possibly **single-electrode resistivity logging**

4. **Right-centre and far right columns**: Mirror the left side structure — likely containing **lithological symbols or pattern fills** (hatching, dotting, or other standard Soviet lithological codes), **formation age designations** (e.g., Палеозой, Мезозой), and possibly **rock description notes**.

5. **No colour** is present — the log is entirely in **black and white / greyscale**, consistent with a photocopied or scanned original blueprint/drawing.

No clear legend box is visible at the resolution provided, though a small boxed annotation appears near the lower-right section of the log (possibly a scale or reference value — **[illegible]**).

## Research Value

This borehole log provides a continuous record of **subsurface lithology and geophysical response** (via SP or resistivity curve) for a specific drill site within the survey area, allowing a geologist to correlate formation boundaries, identify anomalous intervals that may indicate mineralisation or structural features, and calibrate surface geophysical surveys (seismic, gravity, magnetic) against actual drilled stratigraphy. The zones of elevated curve amplitude visible in the lower third of the log are of particular interest as potential indicators of **mineralised, altered, or structurally disrupted rock**. For the broader Report 36572 context, this log would serve as a **ground-truth data point** for interpreting the regional subsurface model and assessing mineral potential at depth beneath the northern Kazakhstan survey area.
